source.title: Metabolic engineering and adaptive laboratory evolution of Kluyveromyces Marxianus for lactic acid production

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid production under low-pH conditions to reduce neutralization costs and enable use of cheap feedstocks.
initial_request.known_context: Current lactic acid production via LAB requires large amounts of neutralization agents and complex nutrients, limiting sustainability. S. cerevisiae is suboptimal due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lactic acid production by LAB requires neutralization and complex nutrients; S. cerevisiae is suboptimal due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a robust, acid-tolerant, Crabtree-negative host for low-pH lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: K. marxianus is a promising alternative due to acid tolerance, fast growth, xylose utilization, and GRAS status.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with K. marxianus as host organism.
evolution_trajectory.observation: K. marxianus is acid tolerant (pH 3-8), can metabolize xylose, and has GRAS status.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a study to exploit K. marxianus diversity and engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: it requires complex and expensive feedstocks and large amounts of neutralization agents for pH control during fermentation.
as a Crabtree-positive yeast, S. cerevisiae tends to favor ethanol production under high sugar concentrations
S. cerevisiae remains a sub-optimal choice for some specific applications where cells are subjected to low pH, as is the case in LA fermentations.
it is acid tolerant with a pH range for growth between 3 and 8

Variants were filtered using DP>40 (depth of coverage greater than 40), FS<40 (Fisher Strand Bias), MQ>40 (mapping quality), MQRankSum >-10, ReadPosRankSum >-8, QUAL>50 (variant quality score), and a variant frequency threshold>12.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that K. marxianus is a suitable host.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify robust strains and engineer them for lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening a diverse collection of K. marxianus strains will identify robust candidates for engineering.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 168 K. marxianus strains, select top 10, engineer for lactic acid production, apply ALE, and validate in bioreactors.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study design includes high-throughput screening, genetic engineering, ALE, and bioreactor validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: we combine different state-of-the-art engineering strategies and the underexplored natural K. marxianus biodiversity to obtain superior cell factories
we screen 168 different K. marxianus strains to identify 10 genetically distinct strains that show superior industrially-relevant phenotypes
we engineer each of the 10 strains to produce LA and further optimize production in the best candidates by using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and process tuning

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Phenotypic diversity and lactic acid tolerance of the strain collection unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High-throughput screening will reveal strains with superior growth under stress conditions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform high-throughput screening on 168 strains under multiple conditions including lactic acid stress.

parameters.conditions: multiple conditions including pH 2.5, 50 g/L lactic acid

evolution_trajectory.observation: Large phenotypic diversity observed; only two strains grew at 50 g/L lactic acid; colonies 18% smaller at pH 2.5 vs pH 3.5.

evidence.quote_or_span: An initial high-throughput screening was performed on 168 K. marxianus strains isolated from diverse environments
To establish a high-throughput screening set-up, the strains were pinned in triplicates on solid medium
Table 2 High-throughput screening conditions
Pictures were taken by the robotic handling system HiTMan
Moreover, the ALE resulted in the identification of a novel mutation in transcription factor IIB Sua7 that improves biomass production under LA stress by 13.5-fold and LA production by 18%.
The best candidate strain, Km3, was subjected to adaptive laboratory evolution, yielding a further 18% increase in LA production, reaching titers of 120 g L $^{-1}$ LA and a yield of 0.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to select a diverse set of robust strains for engineering.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Strains with large colony sizes across conditions are robust and diverse.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 10 strains including benchmark NBRC 1777 based on top 60 colony size in at least one condition.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 10 strains selected, including Km3, Km4, and NBRC 1777.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Genetically engineer selected strains for lactic acid production.

evidence.quote_or_span: in total 10 strains were selected for further engineering (Table 3), including 9 overall robust, diverse strains, and NBRC 1777, a commonly used K. marxianus chassis strain, as a benchmark.
To select the optimal candidate strains, the strains in the top 60 largest colony size for each of the tested conditions were listed.
The selected strains were at least for one condition in the top 60, ensuring a large phenotypic diversity in the strain selection.
Table 3 Criteria for strain selection

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Strains lack lactic acid production pathway.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Deleting PDC1 and CYB2 and integrating LpLDH will enable lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Engineer all 10 strains with pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration, and cyb2Δ using CRISPR/Cas9.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Lactic acid production enabled; best strain Km3 produced 14.8 g/L with yield 0.68 g/g.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Low titers suggest lactic acid tolerance is limiting; hypothesize ALE can improve.

evidence.quote_or_span: LA production was enabled in the 10 selected strains by three genetic modifications introduced by CRISPR/Cas9-based genetic engineering
For the deletion of PDC1 and CYB2, two 90 bp oligonucleotides were designed to be homologous to the regions flanking the genes
Genomic regions were targeted for engineering using a CRISPR/Cas9-gene editing tool for K. marxianus
The best LA-producing strain, $Km3^{pdc1,LDH}$ , $cyb2$ , produced 14.8 g L $^{-1}$ LA with a yield of 0.
5, $Km3^{eng}$ produced 54 g $L^{-1}$ LA, with LA yield of 0.68 g $g^{-1}$ , and overall LA productivity of 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Engineered Km3 produces 14.8 g/L lactic acid.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Lactic acid production likely limited by product toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Lactic acid tolerance is a limiting factor for lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply adaptive laboratory evolution to improve tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on low titers.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform ALE under lactic acid stress.

evidence.quote_or_span: We hypothesized that LA tolerance was a limiting factor for LA production.
The best LA-producing strain, $Km3^{pdc1,LDH}$ , $cyb2$ , produced 14.8 g L $^{-1}$ LA with a yield of 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that lactic acid tolerance limits production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to evolve strains with improved lactic acid tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE under increasing lactic acid concentrations will yield tolerant mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evolve Km3^{eng} in three populations with lactic acid stress and one control without lactic acid.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved populations showed increased growth rate; single colonies isolated for phenotyping.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify causal mutations via whole genome sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: Three populations were evolved in medium supplemented with L-LA and without pH control (A, B, C), while one control population was evolved without LA addition (Control).
After 64, 81, 52 and 104 generations of evolution of populations A, B, C and Control, respectively
Three parallel cultures (A, B, C) were maintained with and one culture without L-LA addition (Control)
Similar to other studies [36, 37], no pH control was applied.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Genetic basis of improved tolerance unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Non-synonymous SNPs in evolved strains contribute to lactic acid tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence best isolates from each population and revert candidate SNPs to wild type.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SUA7 S279P mutation identified as causal for improved tolerance and production; BCK1 V909F also found.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate SUA7 mutation effect on tolerance and production.

evidence.quote_or_span: To identify mutations that influence LA tolerance, we focused on non-synonymous SNPs in protein-coding regions (Table 4) and reverted these SNPs to their wild type nucleotide in the evolved strains.
The best LA-producing isolate for each population was sent for whole genome sequencing.
The genomic DNA was isolated using the Genomic tip-20G kit from QIAGEN. Whole genome Illumina sequencing was performed at BGI Hongkong.
To revert the identified mutations to their wild type nucleotide, gDNAs were designed to specifically target the mutant nucleotide.

To further assess the effect of the identified mutations in conditions that mimic industrial fermentations, strains $Km3^{eng}$ with or without $SUA7^{S279P}$ and $BCK1^{V909F}$ were assessed in a controlled fed-batch bioreactor.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm mutation improves lactic acid tolerance and production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SUA7 S279P enhances lactic acid tolerance and production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test evolved strains and reverse-engineered mutants for growth and production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved strains grew at 30 g/L lactic acid (parental only 10 g/L); 13/24 produced more lactic acid; SUA7 mutation improved acetic acid tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test best strains in bioreactors for industrial relevance.

evidence.quote_or_span: 13 of the 24 strains produced more LA than the non-evolved strain.
the SUA7 mutation also improved tolerance against other organic acids, such as acetic acid
Moreover, the ALE resulted in the identification of a novel mutation in transcription factor IIB Sua7 that improves biomass production under LA stress by 13.5-fold and LA production by 18%.
Instead, in Km3 $^{eng}$ , SUA7 $^{*}$ , Sua7 $^{S279P}$ improved LA tolerance, LA production, maximal LA productivity, acetic acid tolerance and LA production from xylose compared to Km3 $^{eng}$ .

Our hypothesis is that the heterozygous, nonsense PMA1 mutation emerged early in population A. If this is a loss-of-function mutation, less protons would be pumped out of the cells, however, also ATP is conserved. This might have allowed the cells to survive the stress condition and accumulate other mutations, such as the SUA7 homozygous S279P mutation, that were beneficial for LA tolerance.
This might have allowed the cells to survive the stress condition and accumulate other mutations, such as the SUA7 homozygous S279P mutation, that were beneficial for LA tolerance.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SUA7 mutation validated in small scale.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Performance under controlled bioreactor conditions unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fed-batch bioreactor with pH control will yield high lactic acid titers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate Km3^{eng} and Km3^{eng},SUA7* in fed-batch bioreactors at pH 6.0, 4.5, and 3.5.
evolution_trajectory.observation: At pH 6.0: Km3^{eng} 116 g/L, yield 0.83 g/g; Km3^{eng},SUA7* 120 g/L, yield 0.81 g/g. At pH 4.5: 94 g/L. At pH 3.5: 54 g/L.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess limitations and compare to benchmarks.

evidence.quote_or_span: The Infors HT Multifors 6×0.5 L system was used to evaluate the constructed strains further.
The pH was controlled at 6.0 (or 4.5 or 3.5) using 6 M NaOH (VWR), and temperature was maintained at 30 °C throughout the fermentation.
The mass balance was constructed according to the formulas in Figure S2, Supplemental File 2.
Any volume changes due to addition of base and feedstock, and removal of glucose and LA through sampling were considered to calculate the final LA yield and productivity.

Both mutants showed slightly higher LA titers (albeit non-significant), with $Km3^{eng}$ , $SUA7^{*}$ the best variant, with a combination of high LA titer (120 g L $^{-1}$ ), yield (0.81 g g $_{glucose}^{-1}$ ) and maximal productivity (3.
The non-evolved $Km3^{eng}$ reached a final titer of 116 g $L^{-1}$ with a yield of 0.83 g $g^{-1}$ and overall productivity of 1.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Not all evolved strains improved; acetic acid byproduct increased; low-pH production still limited.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Further engineering and process optimization needed for industrial viability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations and compare to LAB benchmarks.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only 13/24 evolved strains improved; acetic acid increased in SUA7* strain; low-pH titers lower; K. marxianus titers lower than LAB but offers cost advantages.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study and suggest future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: 13 of the 24 strains produced more LA than the non-evolved strain.

However, acetic acid production was higher in this strain when compared to the parental strain. Additional engineering, such as introducing the bacterial acetyl-CoA pathway, could be investigated to further improve performance.

While the LA titers achieved in this study are among the highest reported for K. marxianus under aerobic conditions, further improvements in yield and productivity may be needed before the process can be implemented industrially.
the engineered K. marxianus strain offers an advantage regarding nutrient requirements and neutralization costs compared to the bacterial alternatives.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Engineered K. marxianus Km3 with SUA7 S279P mutation achieved 120 g/L lactic acid, yield 0.81 g/g at pH 6.0; low-pH production reached 94 g/L at pH 4.5 and 54 g/L at pH 3.5. Despite lower titers than LAB, K. marxianus offers advantages in nutrient and neutralization costs.
limitations: Only 13/24 evolved strains improved lactic acid production
Acetic acid byproduct increased in SUA7* strain
Titers lower than highest reported LAB production
